title: The Brothers Karamazov — The Three Upayas as Three Brothers
subtitle: Dmitri as Āṇavopāya (action/passion), Ivan as Śāktopāya (purified intellect/rebellion), Alyosha as Śāmbhavopāya (direct love/grace), Zosima as the completed guru, and the devil as the Guardian of the Threshold

summary.one_line: Dostoevsky's final novel read as a complete map of the three upāya-s (the three means to liberation) embodied as the three brothers: Dmitri as Āṇavopāya (the path of action and purifying passion), ...
summary.scope: Covers the entire novel The Brothers Karamazov (1880), with primary focus on the three brothers as embodiments of the three upāya-s, Zosima as the completed guru, Ivan's devil as the Guardian of the Threshold, and Dmitri's dream of the burning barn as the recognition of universal suffering as the work.

body.text: [See Karamazov Books 3, 8-9, and 11. Dmitri is Āṇavopāya — the path of the individual (āṇava = 'pertaining to the atom/individual'), the means that works through action, passion, and the purification of the contracted self through experience. Dmitri lives through the body: his violence, his love for Grushenka, his honour, his despair, his ecstasy. He does not think his way to truth; he burns his way there. The āṇavopāya uses the very instruments of bondage — desire, anger, attachment — as the fuel for liberation. Dmitri's passionate nature is not his flaw but his path: every excess, every explosion, every crash is a wave that erodes the ego. His famous declaration 'I cannot endure that even one man should not be saved' is the āṇavopāya reaching its limit: the passion for the particular (his own salvation) expands into the passion for the universal (the salvation of all). He is the one who must descend into the impure to emerge purified.]

[See Karamazov Books 5, 11 ch.4-9, and 12. Ivan is Śāktopāya — the path of the power of intellect (śakti = power/energy), the means that works through the purification of thought itself. Ivan does not act; he thinks. His rebellion against God is not a rejection of faith but the purification of faith through doubt. The argument of the Grand Inquisitor (Book 5 ch.5) is the śāktopāya at full power: the intellect so refined that it can articulate its own limitation. Ivan's tragedy is that his intellect is pure enough to see the truth but not yet integrated enough to embody it. He is the one who knows but does not do. The devil who appears to him (Book 11 ch.9) is the Guardian of the Threshold — not a demon but the daimon made visible, the figure that appears when the soul reaches the edge of what the intellect can hold. Ivan's madness is the risk of śāktopāya: the intellect that cuts itself free from the heart and cannot find its way back. He must descend into breakdown to break through.]

[See Karamazov Books 1-2, 4, 6-7, and 12. Alyosha is Śāmbhavopāya — the path of the blessed one (śāmbhava = 'pertaining to Śiva'), the means that requires no means, the direct attainment of liberation through grace and love. Alyosha does not struggle; he receives. He is not pure because he has purified himself but because the śaktipāta (the descent of grace) has already done the work before he began. His love is effortless; his faith, through doubt, is immediate. His moment of vision at the stone (Book 12 Epilogue) — when he throws himself on the ground, embraces the earth, and weeps — is the śāmbhavopāya fulfilled: the soul that does not seek God but is found by God. He sees the stars, hears the silence, feels the 'threads from all those infinite worlds of God' linking together in his soul. This is the para-samvit (the supreme consciousness) recognised as the substrate of all experience. Alyosha kisses the earth in love, not in penance. He is the youngest brother because śāmbhavopāya is the culmination — it comes last, even though it was always already there.]

[See Karamazov Book 6. Father Zosima is the completed guru — the one who has traversed all three upāya-s and now transmits from beyond the path. His autobiographical discourse (Book 6 ch.2) is the record of the traversal: he was a young officer of passion (āṇavopāya — the fiery brother), then encountered the mystery of suffering (śāktopāya — the intellectual crisis), and finally received the gift of active love (śāmbhavopāya — the direct knowledge that love is the only law). His teaching is not doctrine but presence: 'Love in action is a harsh and dreadful thing compared to love in dreams.' He knows that the path is not the goal and that teaching is not preaching. His death and the premature corruption of his body — the scandal that shakes the faith of the town — is the guru's final lesson: do not attach to the teacher, do not seek miracles, do not mistake the vessel for the source. The stench of Zosima's corpse is the guru withdrawing his form so the teaching can stand alone.]

[See Karamazov Book 11 ch.9. The devil who visits Ivan is the Guardian of the Threshold — the daimon that appears when the soul has purified itself to the point where it can see its own shadow. This devil is not a fallen angel but a function: 'I am the x in the equation, the necessary premise.' He is Ivan's own intellect personified as a shabby gentleman, a sponger, a failed aesthete. He is not terrifying; he is banal. This is the Guardian of the Threshold in its modern form — not a monster but a mirror. Ivan's devil speaks truths that Ivan himself holds: 'Without suffering, what would become of pleasure?' He is the threshold that appears when the śāktopāya reaches its limit: the intellect, having purified itself of all external authority, must now face itself. Ivan throws a glass at him — the rejection of the vision that the threshold demands. His brain fever is the consequence: the soul that sees the threshold and refuses to cross must fall back into the contraction. The devil laughs and vanishes, but the threshold remains.]

[See Karamazov Book 9 ch.8. Dmitri's dream of the burning barn is the āṇavopāya reaching its culmination — the moment when the soul recognises universal suffering as the work. Arrested for his father's murder (which he did not commit), exhausted, half-delirious, Dmitri dreams of a village in the burnt-out steppe where a mother cries over her starving child. He keeps asking: 'Why are they crying? Why don't they feed the baby?' The dream is not symbolic; it is the direct vision of suffering-as-such, stripped of all narrative. Dmitri, the man of passion, the brother of the body, sees the body of the world in its starvation. His response is not a thought but a cry: 'I cannot endure that even one man should not be saved.' This is the birth of compassion in the soul that has only known desire. The dream is the threshold of the āṇavopāya becoming the śāmbhavopāya: the contraction of the individual passion expands into the infinite compassion that holds all beings. Dmitri accepts punishment for a crime he did not commit — the ultimate āṇavic act: taking on karma as the work, suffering as the path, the barn as the garden.]

gaps: Detailed mapping of each brother's arc to the sub-stages of each upāya needed
Zosima's brother Markel as the pre-guru catalyst could be expanded
Compare Ivan's devil with the daimon tradition's Guardian of the Threshold literature
The trial as the karmic unfolding and the collective soul of the town could be its own passage

issues.title: All passages are meta-commentary — need primary text extraction
issues.description: Passages currently describe the esoteric mapping. Real passages should include actual quotations from the novel with analysis.

issues.title: Fyodor Karamazov as the unregenerate contracted self
issues.description: The father — sensual, cynical, deliberately degraded — is the soul before the path: pure āṇavamala (the impurity of individuation) without any upāya to transform it. Not yet developed.

issues.title: Grushenka and Katerina as śakti figures
issues.description: The two women represent two forms of śakti: Grushenka as the kuṇḍalinī that rises through passion, Katerina as the śakti of duty and pride. Not yet integrated.

issues.title: Smerdyakov as the unintegrated shadow
issues.description: The fourth brother — the epileptic, the murderer, the nihilist — is the shadow that the three upāya-s fail to integrate. He is the contraction that cannot be expanded. Not yet developed.

versions.summary: Initial RO created mapping The Brothers Karamazov onto the three upāya-s of Tantric Śaivism. Six passages covering Dmitri as Āṇavopāya, Ivan as Śāktopāya, Alyosha as Śāmbhavopāya, Zosima as the completed guru, Ivan's devil as the Guardian of the Threshold, and Dmitri's barn dream as the recognition of universal suffering.

timeline.event: RO created in draft state with 1 primary source and 6 passages
